coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon oxide quantum dots for flexible humidity sensor: Experimental and SCC-DFTB study
coredata.dc:creator: Kondee, Sarawut
Arayawut, Onsuda
Pon-On, Weeraphat
Wongchoosuk, Chatchawal
coredata.dc:description: A flexible resistive-type humidity sensor based on N-type nitrogen-doped carbon oxide quantum dots (NCQDs) is presented. The NCQDs were synthesized by using simple hydrothermal method with reactions via citric acid and urea. The UV–vis, FE-TEM and XPS analysis confirm a successful doping of N atoms and existence of oxygen-containing functional groups into carbon quantum dots with the homogeneous spherical structure. The NCQDs humidity sensor exhibits high humidity response, good repeatability, and high stability as well as linear response to humidity in the range of 20–90% RH. The humidity sensing mechanism has been in-depth investigated by self-consistent charge density functional tight-binding (SCC-DFTB) including dispersion interaction. The SCC-DFTB reveals an important role of N doping with physisorption via direct charge transfer sensing mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Humidity sensor
Carbon oxide quantum dots
Flexible sensor
SCC-DFTB

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of flexible humidity NQCDs sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kasetsart University Research and Development Institute
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Kasetsart University Research and Development Institute

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Kasetsart University Research and Development Institute (KURDI) under the grant number FF(KU) 25.64 . S.K. acknowledges the Graduate Program Scholarship from the Graduate School, Kasetsart University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams of (a) NCQDs synthesis and (b) a flexible NCQDs humidity sensor fabrication process.
Schematic diagram of humidity-sensing experimental setup.

Adsorption of water molecules at different sites of NCQD; (a) H of water interacts to C of NCQD (HH2O-CNCQD), (b) H of water interacts to N of NCQD (HH2O-NNCQD), (c) H of water interacts to functional N–H of NCQD at edge (HH2O-NHNCQD), (d) H of water interacts to functional O–H of NCQD at edge (HH2O-OHNCQD), (e) O of water interacts to C of NCQD (OH2O-CNCQD), (f) O of water interacts to N of NCQD (OH2O-NNCQD), (g) O of water interacts to functional N–H of NCQD at edge (OH2O-NHNCQD) and (h) O of water interacts to functional O–H of NCQD at edge (OH2O-OHNCQD).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum (b,c) FE-TEM images and (d) size distribution of synthesized NCQDs.
XPS spectra of the synthesized NCQDs: (a) survey scan, (b) the high-resolution O 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) C 1s.
Changes in resistance of the flexible NCQDs humidity sensor to dry air (10% RH) with (a) 95% RH and (b) different %RH. (c) Linear relationship of % RH and its resistance. (d) Flexible properties.
(a) Side and (b) top views of the optimized NCQD structure based on SCC-DFTB method. (c) NCQD-H2O interaction energies at different adsorption configurations.
Comparison of NCQDs humidity sensor with previously published works.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon oxide quantum dots for flexible humidity sensor: Experimental and SCC-DFTB study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok, 10900, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok, 10900, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A flexible resistive-type humidity sensor based on N-type nitrogen-doped carbon oxide quantum dots (NCQDs) is presented. The NCQDs were synthesized by using simple hydrothermal method with reactions via citric acid and urea. The UV–vis, FE-TEM and XPS analysis confirm a successful doping of N atoms and existence of oxygen-containing functional groups into carbon quantum dots with the homogeneous spherical structure. The NCQDs humidity sensor exhibits high humidity response, good repeatability, and high stability as well as linear response to humidity in the range of 20–90% RH. The humidity sensing mechanism has been in-depth investigated by self-consistent charge density functional tight-binding (SCC-DFTB) including dispersion interaction. The SCC-DFTB reveals an important role of N doping with physisorption via direct charge transfer sensing mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: During the last two decades, carbon-based nanomaterials including fullerenes, carbon nanotube, graphene have received huge attention as an emerging electronic material for advanced science and technology [1,2]. Carbon quantum dot (CD) is a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials having zero-dimensional nanoparticle with size less than 10 nm. In recent year, CDs have received increasing attention in many applications such as electrocatalysis [3], optoelectronic devices [4], photocatalytic activity [5], supercapacitors [6] and biomedical applications [7] due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, tunable electrical and optical properties, high stability and cost-effectiveness. The CDs have also used as nanocomposite material for gas sensing applications due to enhancement of electrons transfer capabilities [8,9]. However, pristine CDs exhibited low sensitive to gases and humidity detection at room temperature [10,11]. The pure CDs were almost unresponsive to relative humidity from 7% to 95% [10]. The CDs can be synthesized by several methods including ultrasonic process [12], laser ablation technique [13], microwave irradiation [14,15] and hydrothermal [16–18] with different precursors such as ethylenediamine, thiourea, phenylenediamine and carbonaceous sources [19,20]. Among them, the hydrothermal method has become a most popular one because it offers low-cost production, simple operating process, low energy consumption, and provides a high quantity of quantum dots [17,21].

Humidity play an important role for human health because it directly affects heat transfer via evaporation of sweat into the air. Too much or too low humidity can cause health issues such as damage of tracheal mucosa, chronic skin and throat irritation, altered blood circulation, high risk of cold and flu, increased asthma and allergy problems and heatstroke which can be fatal [22–25]. For examples, human feels much hotter when relative humidity (%RH) of the air is high. It makes sweat out as much as for heat exhaustion leading to dehydration and chemical imbalances within the body. In case of extremely low %RH, water molecules in skin quickly evaporate and disrupt the natural moisture balances causing damage of human organ. Therefore, real-time monitoring of the humidity surrounding the human body is necessary. The key of humidity detection for healthcare application is flexible humidity sensor.

A variety of nanomaterials has been used as sensing films for flexible humidity sensors. For examples, K. Lazarova et al. [26] employed poly(vinyl alcohol-co-vinyl acetal) with acetal content of 24% for %RH detection in the range 5–95%. L. Ma et al. [27] fabricated a high performance of capacitive-type humidity sensor based on carbon-gold nanoparticles deposited on silk protein substrate. Z. Zhuang et al. [28] synthesized LiCl-Doped Poly (Ether Ether Ether Ketone)–Co-Poly (Ethylene Glycol) copolymers and showed the outstanding humidity sensing properties. X. Ni et al. [29] presented humidity sensing properties of quaternary ammonium salt of 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate/methyl methacrylate. X. Li et al. [30] demonstrated a self-powered humidity sensor based on polypyrrole modified melamine aerogel. Although, many nanomaterials provide high humidity sensing performances, most flexible humidity sensors rely on polymer-based sensing materials and capacitive-type humidity sensor. These may limit long-term stability and capacitive-type humidity sensor needs the complicated circuit to readout the signal resulting in a difficult integration with other flexible electronic devices and high power consumption for its practical wearable applications.

Based on the benefits of biocompatible CDs, doping of high electronegativities N atoms and oxygen-containing functional groups may help to increase the water interactions. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon oxide quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method and used for sensing material of the flexible resistive-type humidity sensor. The humidity sensing mechanism has been in-depth investigated via self-consistent charge density functional tight-binding (SCC-DFTB) method including dispersion interaction.

The NCQDs were synthesized via a modified hydrothermal method [31] as shown in Fig. 1 a. 1 g of citric acid monohydrate (
C 6
H 8
O 7
*
H 2
O ,
m o l a r
m a s s
o f
210.14
g / m o l )
and 2 g of urea (C
H 4
N 2
O ,
molecular weight of 60.06 g/mol) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized (DI) water and then stirred for 30 min. The mixture was heated in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 180
°C
for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was treated with centrifugation for 45 min and washed with ethanol and DI, respectively, for 3 times and then freeze dried for 6 h. The NCQDs sample was received and kept in the DI water at room temperature for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of flexible humidity NQCDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The fabrication of the flexible humidity sensor based on NCQDs is displayed in Fig. 1b. The silver interdigitated electrodes were screen printed on a transparent plastic substrate (PET) and then dried at room temperature. 100
μ L
of NCQDs solution was dropped via a micropipette onto silver interdigitated electrodes and then the sensor was kept at room temperature for 24 h.

The humidity sensing measurements were done via our lab-made dynamic flow system. Schematic diagram of humidity-sensing experimental setup is displayed in Fig. 2 . The measurements were performed at room temperature (26
±
2
° C )
in the RH ranging from 10% to 95%. The NCQDs humidity sensor was installed in our designed 36 ml Teflon chamber. The desired humidity was generated via the water vapor placed in a temperature-controlled heat bath and adjusting two mass flow controllers. The total flow rate was fixed at 200 SCCM. First, the dry air through the silica gel was flowed into the sensor chamber for 4 min to stabilize humidity background and used as the base line. Next, the humidified air generated from the bubbling system was introduced into the sensor chamber. The resistance of NCQDs humidity sensor was measured by a simple voltage divider circuit with a constant applied voltage of 10 V. The data were recorded every second via our LabVIEW program with a NI USB DAQ 6008.

The SCC-DFTB method is based on second-order expansion of the DFT energy with respect to density fluctuations around a reference density [32,33]. It should be noted that the SCC-DFTB method can predict structural and electronic properites in excellent agreement with DFT methods but ∼100–1000 times faster as well as consistent with experimental observations [34,35]. The NCQD was modeled and fully optimized by SCC-DFTB including dispersion interaction with pbc-0-3 parameter set [36]. The models of water moleculte adsorption on various sites of NCQDs surface are displayed in Fig. 3 . Firsly, the NCQD and water molecule were fully optimized without constraints until the atomic forces were less than 1 × 10−4 Hartree/Bohr. The SCC tolerance was set to 10−6 au. The electron temperature was kept at 300 K in order to improve SCC convergence and include the effect of thermal electronic excitation [37,38]. Various Slater-Koster parameters sets such as matsci-0-3, mio-1-1, and pbc-0-3 were tested for this system and the pbc-0-3 was selected due to achieve the lowest total energy. The SCC-DFTB calculations also include the dispersions based on Lennard Jones parameters. The energy gap was estimated from the difference of HOMO and LUMO. The interactions were calculated according to the equation:
Eint = Etot(NCQD + water molecule) - Etot(NCQD) - Etot(water molecule)
Where Etot (NCQD + water molecule) is total energy of NCQD with water molecule, Etot
(NCQD) and Etot (water molecule) are total energy of NCQD and water molecule, respectively.

The XPS measurements (AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos analytical, Manchester UK) were performed to delineate the chemical composition and the nature of bonding of NCQDs. The NCQDs were deposited on alumina substrate without any metal coating for XPS analysis. The base pressure in the XPS chamber was about 5 × 10−9 torr. The samples were excited with X-ray hybrid mode 700 × 300 μm spot area with a monochromatic Al Kα1,2 radiation at 1.4 keV. The X-ray anode was run at 15 kV and 10 mA. The photoelectrons were detected with a hemispherical analyzer positioned at an angle of 90° with respect to the normal to the sample surface. The spectra were calibrated using the C1s line (BE = 285 eV) [39]. It should be noted that adventitious carbon and substrate can make the significant shifting of binding energy for C 1s spectrum resulting in an incorrect spectral interpretation. The binding energy may be corrected in order to achieve a high accuracy of chemical bonding determination [40–43]. As the adventitious carbon was used for charge referencing in this work, reported binding energy values should be treated with caution.

The UV–visible spectrum of NCQDs exhibits two well-defined absorption peaks as shown in Fig. 4 a. The first peak (∼268 nm) is attributed to the
π →
π ∗
transition of CN or CC bond [44,45] whereas the second peak (∼334 nm) corresponds to the
n →
π ∗
transition of CO or CN bond [46] indicating the successful doping of N atoms at the edges or defects of CQDs. The formation mechanism of NCQDs relies on carbonation and surface passivation during hydrothermal treatment [47]. The citric acid used as carbon seeds was decomposed and polymerized into small dots. With addition of urea and hydrothermal at a high temperature, carbonization starts to form the carbon core with N doping. The amino groups from urea act as surface passivation to form NCQDs. Field-emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM, JEM-3100F (300 kV)) was used to investigate the morphology and compositional elements of NCQDs. The synthesized NCQDs have spherical shape as shown in Fig. 4b–d. The average diameter of NCQDs is 7.00 ± 0.93 nm that reveals high homogeneous NCQDs size and shape. Based on an EDS analysis, the NCQDs consist C, N and O atoms with % weight of 75.47%, 5.52% and 19.01%, respectively, as shown in Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information.

As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the survey scan of XPS spectrum of NCQDs exhibits three dominant C 1s, N 1s and O 1s peaks with the binding energy of 258.0 eV, 400.0 eV and 532.0 eV, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 5b) is composed of four peaks at 531.3 eV, 532.4 eV, 533.7 eV and 534.9 eV assigned to the form of CO, (CO*)OH, –OH and C–O–C [48], respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) can be deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 400.1 eV and 401.3 eV which represent Pyrrolic-N and N–H [49,50], respectively. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 5d) reveals five peaks at 285.0 eV, 285.9 eV, 286.8 eV, 288.0 eV and 289.0 eV corresponding to C/CC, C–N [51,52], C–O [53,54], CO [55,56] and O–CO [57], respectively. Based on XPS results, they confirm the doping N atoms and existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl function group on sp2 carbon quantum dots structure. The model of a NCQD molecule was thus constructed as displayed in Fig. 3 and used for theoretical study of interactions with water molecule via the SCC-DFTB.

The flexible NCQDs humidity sensor was tested by switching between dry air (10% RH) and high humidity (95% RH) for ten loops as shown in Fig. 6 a. It clearly exhibits as n-type conducting behaviour since the high resistance at dry air (10% RH) rapidly decreases with the adsorption of electron-donating water molecules at high humidity (95% RH). The average humidity sensor response was calculated to be ∼90.97% with small SD of 1.8% upon ten cycles suggesting a high humidity sensor respone and good repeatability. It should be noted that most of graphene or carbon quantum dots display p-type conducting behaviour. However, it can be exhibited as n-type conducting behaviour depending on edges and doping effects [58]. The dynamic resistances of flexible NCQDs humidity sensor on different % RH are shown in Fig. 6b. The resistances decrease to the desired % RH with the different times, i.e., changing from 10% RH to 50% RH within 11 s suggesting a fast response time. However, a long response time can be found at high humidity (90% RH) due to the effect of long generating time for high water vapor into sensor chamber in the measurement system. The resistance of flexible NCQDs humidity sensor decreases linearly as well (R2 = 0.985) in the range of 20–90% RH as displayed in Fig. 6c. At too low humidity (<20% RH), intrinsic conductance behavior of NCQDs (high resistance) is dominant whereas too high humidity (>90% RH), available water adsorption sites are limited leading to non-linear relationship at too high/low humidity.

To investigate the flexible properties, the NCQDs humidity sensor was bent at various angles (20°, 40° and 60°). The NCQDs humidity sensor with the fixed bending angles was exposed to 70% RH and recovered to baseline at 20% RH due to good linear relationship condition. As shown in Fig. 6d, the bending angle at 20° did not significantly affect on humidity sensing properties. One can be observed that only 20% RH gives a fully recovery to baseline. It suggests a high performance in real-world application. The humidity responses seem to be increased at bending angle of 40° and 60°. The average humidity responses at 40° and 60° upon five loops are ∼67.59% and ∼71.59%, respectively. A possible explanation may be explained based on an increase of adsorption porous sites. Under high bending extension, the flexible sensing film increases the active interfacial sites for water adsorption leading to increase the humidity responses. Similar behavious can be found with other sensing nanomaterials [45,59]. The stability tests were done during 30 days. The NCQDs humidity sensor was kept at normal environment (room temperature with ∼50–55 %RH) and exposed between dry air (10% RH) and 95% RH for ten cycles every 3 days. The average humidity sensor respones upon 30 days were illustrated as shown in Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information. No significant shifting of humidity sensor respones was found during 30 days, confirming its high stability for humidity sensing. A comparison of our NCQDs humidity sensor with other sensing nanomaterials [60–66] is displayed in Table 1 . It reveals that the NCQDs material possess superior humidity sensor response than other sensing nanomaterials. Moreover, the NCQDs owns the flexibility that can be applied in a wearable device.

To study the influence of residual gas contamination, the NCQDs humidity sensor was exposed to several gases such as O2 (95%), CO (100 ppm), CO2 (1000 ppm) and NH3 (100 ppm). Average sensor responses of the NCQDs humidity sensor from three measurements have been shown in Fig. S3 in the Supporting Information. The average sensor responses to O2, CO, and CO2 are less than 1% suggesting no serious effects of NCQDs sensor on humidity determination. However, average sensor response toward NH3 was calculated to be ∼2.95% at concentration of 100 ppm. It refers a limitation of NCQDs sensor to investigate the humidity at high NH3 concentration in an environment. Significant changing of NCQDs resistance to NH3 may cause from interactions of hydroxyl function group [58].

The optimized NCQD structure based on SCC-DFTB method is displayed in Fig. 7 a and b. Total atoms of NCQD model are 156 atoms compositing of C (103 atoms), H (42 atoms), N (2 atoms) and O (9 atoms). N doping position and the edge function groups into CD structure were modeled based on chemical bonding determination from XPS results. It should be noted that the synthesized NCQDs can be assumed as a single-crystalline structure (Fig. S4 in the Supporting Information) and no grain boundaries due to the small size of ∼7 nm. Moreover, no significant segregation of any atoms was found in the synthesized NCQDs (see the results from TEM/EDS mapping in Fig. S4 in the Supporting Information). The O can form at the surfaces of NCQD only. The average interlayer distance is ∼3.416 Å according to the experimental observation and calculation of interlayer distance in graphite [67,68]. The NCQD structure exhibits the AB stacking order without any distortion. It refers to high stability of the NCQD structure.

The interaction energies between NCQD and water molecule at various sites are displayed in Fig. 7c. At the optimum points, the interaction energies of the HH2O-CNCQD, HH2O-NNCQD, HH2O-NHNCQD, HH2O-OHNCQD, OH2O-CNCQD, OH2O-NNCQD, OH2O-NHNCQD, OH2O-OHNCQD configurations are −0.085, −0.0748, −0.037, −0.0935, −0.0565, −0.127, −0.102 and −0.0404 eV, respectively, indicating physisorption of water molecule on NCQD via Van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding. All interaction energies as a function of distances for all configurations are listed in Table S1 in the Supporting Information. It should be noted that chemisorption such as ionic bonding and covalent bonding is not dominant in this case because the interaction energies are less than 0.2 eV. A benefit of physisorption is long-team stability as shown in Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information. At normal environment, the water molecule can't penetrated into NCQDs and make strong chemisorption causing humidity sensor respones shifting at the long time usage. The OH2O-NNCQDs configuration exhibits the highest interaction energy at the binding distance of 3.1 Å. The O atom of water molecule prefers to orient towards the N atoms of NCQD. At this adsorption site, electrons charge transfers from water molecule to NCQD (0.026 e) and the Fermi energy level shifts towards conduction band (from −3.298 eV to −3.229 eV) with energy gap reduction (from 1.617 eV to 1.609 eV) according to the experimental result of n-type conducting NCQD behaviour. The interaction energies for selective configurations were also calculated with a popular DFT method like B3LYP/6-31G* as shown in Fig. S5 in the Supporting Information. The calculated results clearly show a good consistency between SCC-DFTB and B3LYP/6-31G* for investigation of optimum distance and interaction energy. The SCC-DFTB can predict a higher interaction energy than the B3LYP/6-31G* because it includes dispersion interactions like a van der Waals interaction. In addition, the SCC-DFTB was proved to estimate the absolute interaction energy values between PBE and B3LYP since its parameters combine quantities from both functionals [37]. These results confirm a reliability of the SCC-DFTB for sensing mechanism investigation.

A flexible NCQDs humidity sensor was synthesized by hydrothermal followed by simple drop coating method. The UV–vis and XPS analysis confirmed the existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl function groups as well as doping N atoms on the surfaces of carbon quantum dots. The FE-TEM clearly exhibited homogeneous spherical structures of NCQDs with the size of ∼7 nm. The humidity sensing results indicated high sensor response (∼90.97%) from 10% RH to 95% RH, high repeatability, high stability over 30 days, good linear dependant in the humidity range of 20–90% RH and independence with the residual gas contaminations such as O2, CO and CO2 at room temperature. A flexible NCQDs humidity sensor showed no any effect on bending angle (<20°) in which more bending angle (>20°) improved the humidity sensing response due to increase of adsorption porous sites that is very useful for wearable application. The SCC-DFTB study revealed a high stability AB stacking structure of NCQDs with the average interlayer distance of ∼3.416 Å. Based on the interaction energies between NCQD and water molecule, the water molecules can adsorb on NCQD at all sites via physisorption whereas the highest interaction relied on orientation of O atoms of water towards N atoms of NCQD suggesting an important role of N doping. Based on results, we anticipate that NCQDs can facilitate new resistive-type humidity sensing material for future practical wearable applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Kasetsart University Research and Development Institute (KURDI) under the grant number FF(KU) 25.64. S.K. acknowledges the Graduate Program Scholarship from the Graduate School, Kasetsart University.
